title: A matter of identity: race and class issues in American urban schools
abstract: Two new books, Class reunion and Urban schools, offer significant contributions to the field of qualitative research and the knowledge base of urban educational theory. Both books are based on ethnographic case studies played out against the backdrop of changing social, economic and policy issues in the postmodern knowledge economy. Each book focuses on a different section of the traditionally underserved and underrepresented student population in American urban high schools. In Class reunion, Lois Weis uses a feminist and arguably middle-class lens to examine identity formation among white working-class students in the post-industrial age. Urban Schools' authors Mickey Lauria and Luis Mir6n study identity development among African Americans in four inner-city schools from an avowedly critical theorist perspective. They begin with the postulation that disadvantaged African American children are being socialized, with the help of the education system, to fill lower-level jobs in the service industry. Like Weis they are also interested in how the educational demands of the knowledge economy are reshaping students' underlying social values. journal: British Journal of Sociology of Education
